sentences.text: They didn't know what the Russian communist system was, but soon enough, they learned.
They didn't know privacy.
Everything was belonged to the government.
People started a different life, altogether.
Like for Poland, there were more democracy.
You could say what you want, go someplace, travel from one town and back again, but with Russians was restrictions.
You couldn't go travel anyplace without permission.
What they promised to bring in from Moscow material or Kiev or everything did in the beginning.
They start rationing, and people has to stay in the line all night to wait to get the piece of bread or to buy a pair of shoes.
And sometimes it used to take a whole family sleeping two hours, and this would stay in line to be the first in line, because still you get in the line, wasn't anything leftover.
So my family had a way out.
I don't know.
They start to have-- shoe store.
My mother was a great cook, like I tell you before.
She opened a restaurant.
In the shoe store we did open a restaurant.
And the Russian government looked away, because the soldiers for the army, they baked, and they get good prices.
Even they bought the flour and everything on black market.
They looked away, just to support their army.
And it was good for us too.
But my uncle had a grocery store, and he has to be a guard in a warehouse.
So they didn't like the regime.
People had hotels, and they have to go work dig ditches.
They took away-- no privatization.
They were against the Russian system.
And also a lot of refugees from Poland came over to us.
And they told us already, better trust-- trust us what happened-- the Germans doing on the other side of the river to the Polish Jews.

And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my father and mother, they still didn't believe it, because they had remember in the First World War that the Austrian Germans, being occupied by them, there was more generous, gentle people.
And people were just told what's going on.
Trust us, this is what they're doing.
They couldn't believe it.
This is just a modern country like this should think like this.
So nobody believed.
And a lot of people was waiting the Germans should come in, even.
They figured it was their salvation.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so the young people, they're supposed to belong to the communist parties, the [NON-ENGLISH] pioneers and everything.
Like me-- they tried to take me and have somebody to sponsor me to be a pioneer.
I have to lie that my father was a farmer, not a businessman.
A businessman is a [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], means he's a rich man.
Bourgeois.
And it cannot be by this.
And so somebody has to sponsor but his parents was working class.
And I meet them, the family, we were growing.
My sister was big.
And she want to learn a profession-- bookkeeping.
She went to the city of Lwow before the war.
My other brothers, they were out learning professions.
A lot of people were sent away to Siberia for saying anything against the Russian regime.
They send them away.
And my father had this-- this restaurant business.
And the mother was cooking.
And we even helped my grandmother to open a bakery.
With this we made-- made good money for them.
In the last months already, we felt something will go on.
The Russians wind up-- to make lists-- everybody from the people who are used to have a business or suspicious, they wanted to evacuate and send them away.
And my father was on the list.
And German spies already were in the town.
And the Ukraine collaborators, they gave him hideout.
Openly they used to march around the to find out the layout in the city.
So we knew something.
The war is coming.
We see the movement from the Russian Army, going to the front, coming back from the front.
And we used to ask them.
They said-- they said, us?
Nobody can beat us.
Then came the 1941 surprise in June [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 22nd, 1941.
I remember when early in the morning, planes flew up, and cannons shooting, and the war broke out.
And the radio said that the war is-- but this was a song.
They-- they-- they bombard Kiev, and they let us know that the water has start is the translation in English.
Yeah.
[NON-ENGLISH SINGING] But in the beginning, is [NON-ENGLISH] they mention the day too.
[NON-ENGLISH SINGING] This means that hour they bombard Kiev, they let us know that the war has began.
Even though we were by the border, when the Polish-- I mean Russian German border, but they didn't come around to our town three days later.
They surrounded us from there.
They were-- they went in different ways.
They left the whole army-- the Russian army-- behind.
They surrounded us.
We were-- weren't thinking.
We were-- we weren't thinking just about the Germans.
We weren't thinking about this.
We were thinking just to survive the bombarding.
In the meantime, me, my family, moved the whole house for the-- from probably from 1400 where the walls was four feet thick, because they figured it's safe from shooting and bombarding.
And after three days, they came in.
And I was there with my family and one from the neighbors, name is [NON-ENGLISH].
And I will never forget it.
When the German in-- in the house-- they come in with machine guns.
This was the rabbi's son, named Moshe.
And the stepped on the rabbi's son, Moshe.
He was davening, praying, and everything.
There was a very religious woman-- Simi Armbender.
She took the rabbi's talus off his head, and she beg it, and she cried the words, [NON-ENGLISH] Israel.
Is unbelievable.
Only you have to be an artist to describe the moment of that.
And everybody was crying and crying.
And the SS men came in.
I saw my mother ask them if we can go home, back to our home, because we didn't eat for two days, three days, we can bake.
He gave us permission to go back.
And the first time in my life I saw dead men-- Jewish man with a wired beard laying in the middle of the street, dead.
I don't know, but as we near a temple and this was a time when I really saw that person a shot in my life.
Yeah.
When I walked with my mother.
There's still cannons shooting and bullets flying from all over till I come to my-- to our home.
And so we used to bake our bread and everything, so my mother put the wood in the oven to bake the bread and everything.
Meantime, the Germans coming into the house.
And this was one pump really-- a water pump-- behind, because in our time, Poland wasn't a water pump, only when the Russian came, they built a water pump.
So the Germans took me out to pump water when there was shooting and the cannons and everything.
I was amazed.
I was not afraid.
I was forced to pump the water.
I was so tired.
And I wait the minutes should-- it was dark, and they released me.
They let me go in back to my house.
They were bathing themselves, washing themselves, because-- and I was pumping all the time, the water.
Then we had a bicycle at home.
And they took it.
They come in.
They took it away.
The following-- the following day is the Russians' counterattacked.
And there was shooting.
And our town was a lot of wooden houses with straw roofs.
So the town-- the whole town-- went on fire.
The Germans also at that time find up all the Jews, and they closed them in stores.
In the market was a lot of stores.
They locked them in the markets, the men, because on the fire, they release them to put out the fire.
Then my father was running.
We had cows, a farm, everything.
He went to the farm to give the cows-- to feed them.
The Germans start yelling to him, halt, halt halt.
He was escaping.
And he jumped over a fence-- eight foot.
And I was amazed in that.
For me, at that time, a man was-- like my father was 49 years old, 48 years-- we called him an old man.
And when I tell this story to somebody, when I was 48 years old, I felt like 20 years old.
And at that time I was amazed to see him jumping over a fence.
And he escaped from the Germans that time.
No.
Then he come to the-- he came to the house, because it was around-- like a small town.
It's not far.
He came to the house, and he told the story.
So it is what I know.
Anyway, the Germans settled, and they occupied the town.
It was established.
Right away, they established Ukrainian police, volunteer police.
They made the Jewish Judenrat.
And [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] assigned people to work.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And after a couple days laws came out to bring in gold, silver.
Everything we have to turn over that is not allowed.
And we have to wear the armband with the blue star.
And a week later, they called.
They said from age 16 to 60-something, everybody has to appear in the market.
So my-- my father didn't want to appear.
He went out of town, opposite, to hide himself in the cemetery.
On the way was Moshe Weingart, another businessman Father knew.
He was going to market.
So my father tell him, don't go.
He wouldn't listen, and he went.
So the Germans took out four people from all the group and took them to the little mountain there and shot them.
And this man was between them.
Where you with your father when he was hiding in the cemetery?
No.
I didn't go.
I wasn't-- because I wasn't 16 and 15.
I was underage, so I didn't have to go.
I didn't have to appear.
I was with my mother in the house.
And this-- we lived in the market.
I saw this whole thing.
I saw.
No, the shooting I didn't see.
I saw when after they let the list of people, the Germans allowed them-- only people-- they allowed to really to bury in the cemetery.
They'd be doing a favor.
They said get punished because they cut off their telephone lines.
That's why they punish them.
They shouldn't do it anymore.
This was beginning in this town for the killing-- was killing.
But I saw the people.
I walked over to the mountain.
I saw them.
Beneath the mountain I saw these four people.
But I knew the people too.
Yes.
This was-- this was 1941.
Then people were assigned work different works.
Me, they took me out to clean the barrels from the Russian army.
Coming back home, I stole a revolver.
It was dangerous.
I didn't think.
I don't know.
Whatever I did, I had no-- who guide me to do?
I don't know.
Because it was warehouses for weapon.
And I-- we were supposed to clean it, put it in crates and sweeping and everything.
I was with a friend of mine.
And I took this revolver.
I took it back to the-- like, the ghetto.
The ghetto wasn't surrounded it was open ghetto, not with barbed wire, because it was the beginning.
And then I took this revolver in a big house in a cellar what was open in the [INAUDIBLE] in the cellar.
And I hid it between the bricks.
And I told my friend not to tell anybody.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I-- then-- yeah, then I was assigned to work with the railroad.
I used to load and unload the railroad.
When I was with the railroad, I learned-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] each cargo had an assignment-- where and what direction it goes.
I didn't know what is east, what is west.
And believe me, I had to say I don't know.
I didn't know.
Even we learned in school, I don't remember.
No, I don't know, but what side of the street.
I didn't think about this.
But I knew one thing, like, I never went anyplace.
I knew to the right the railroad, the thing's going to Germany.
To left, they're going to Russia.
This is what I know, because it was-- it was near the lines.
It was right to left.
So I learned one thing to have in mind already.
In case I have to escape, I'll escape with the railroad closer to the front-- the Russian front.
And then if they-- they liberate the Russians, and the Germans will retreat, I'll be liberated.
This was my way to think to save myself.
I didn't think-- I didn't know what's going on later.
Partisans, partisans-- I didn't know anything, this.
So-- and I used to go to work every-- every day in the morning.
In the night, they'd bring us back.
I saw a lot of [NON-ENGLISH], where they used to do beating people, shooting people.
German-- the Germans released prisoners of war, the Ukrainian.
And they used to stop by the station, and they used to say, kill the Jewish and save-- save Russia.
This was [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
And then another used to say, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
If they're Jewish, they're dead.
Get them out.
They don't have right to go home, because they figured somebody, you know, pretend that he's not Jewish.
I saw this.
I know what trouble we getting already.
Else, I can't tell you everything.
I'll have to skip a lot of things.
So I'll tell your more what is important to this story.
I worked in the railroad.
And then one day, my sister was-- she escaped to [NON-ENGLISH] to study.
Came on back.
Instead to retreat with Russians, she came back.
She wanted to be with the family.
We were happy to see her.
And one of-- the opposite one, and my older brother for me, he retreat to Russia.
So we figured he'll survive.
We were crying of them.
But then we saw what's going on.
We were happy what he escaped.
He'll maybe the only one to survive.
Chaim.
After a while, we find out that he died from hunger, from sickness, and everything.
And my sister, which she did a stupid thing, instead to escape with the friends, but they survived. they retreat to Russia.
And she came back and she-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] yeah, she saw my father.
Couple days after she came back, a truck appeared in the front of my house, when I used to live there.
And they round up people-- only men.
A couple hours before this, my father told me to take the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] Sefer Torah.
This is a big story of this, like I told you before, that my grandmother was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] commit herself to fast.
The same thing-- my father promised if he live, the first child will live, he will write a Sefer Torah.
So he wrote a Sefer Torah for three years.
For three years, he kept the people to write the story, make the skin, and everything, feed them and pay them for three years.
So it was a big Sefer Torah in honor of my father, given to the shul.
But we wouldn't go already pray in shul, by then Germans-- we couldn't go because we was in ghetto.
They wouldn't let us out.
So he made me to sneak in to take out the torah from the temple and bring it in our house.
And I hid it.
And it was between the walls in my home.
So when the truck came in there, rounding up people, my father told me to go bring up the Sefer Torah.
So the mother said to my father, maybe wait a little, because we used to dry the laundry the same thing in the attic.
Used to dry the laundry, and I'm finishing with the laundry, so you go together.
In the same time, German-- the Ukraine Kowalchuk, what I was a witness for him, took out my father and put him in the truck.
I ran over close to my father and ask him, what going to be?
He said to me, go away.
They'll take you too, in Yiddish, in Jewish.
So I walked away, and this is the last time I was saw him.
[CRYING] [SOBBING] I'm sorry.
Then-- then they took him to the headquarters for the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] for the Germans.
And they had there-- had there two lines for people.
They ask what kind of profession.
[CRYING]
So my-- my father was staying in one line, and my brother with my uncle-- my mother's brother-- stand the other side-- one side.
So the business people-- the people on one side-- and the working people on the left side.
So my brother thought he'll stay with the father, because the business people will let them go, because the working people they maybe accuse them as communists.
So he jumped over to my-- to my father's side.
They took him out-- [SOBBING] out of town near the cemetery, about 400 people, and they shot them.
I followed.
I followed the trucks to the cemetery.
The Ukraine police surrounded the place and wouldn't let anybody through.
But it was on the other side and from the cemetery I heard the shooting.
And the trucks came empty.
And the Germans denied.
They said they didn't shoot them.
They sent them away for work.
Meantime, they collected liquor, and drink.
They got drunk and wild.
And [NON-ENGLISH] Germans from the town had a girlfriend.
And one them-- dignitaries-- [NON-ENGLISH] with their big farms.
So we went to them.
So she was a girlfriend for this [NON-ENGLISH].
She said, if you give me so much and so much money, I'll find out for you.
So every time [INAUDIBLE]-- every time we would give them more and more money.
[INAUDIBLE] oh, she's gone to Germany.
She's going to bring letters back-- never happened.
So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I went there, and I find pieces from the armband, pieces of flesh.
The graves flat.
You shouldn't recognize anything.
But even it was true, the people didn't want to believe it's true.
They still looked for some-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to calm themselves down and everything.
My mother went to this lady, and every time just taking out more money, more money, more money.
Then my mother came over to me and my little brother, and hugged us together.
And she was praying, I wish I can at least bring you-- I should live to bring you to my-- her son-- the brother-- to Palestine.
Yeah, and she hoped.
She already lived for this.
She knew already the older brother was killed and is so-- After-- a month later, the same thing-- another-- another grabbing.
They call this [SPEAKING UKRAINIAN].
[SPEAKING UKRAINIAN] is being grabbed.
Is a Ukrainian word.
They grabbed-- they grabbed people in the street.
Come-- same thing for truck and same thing-- but before they had this Kowalchuk, the Ukraine commandant was known to my mother.
Ask them when I tell you the story that my father told me to go away from him.
He went in-- the policeman, the commandant-- asking for shovels.
So he asked him, why you need shovels?
He said, we're taking him out to work.
But with the shovels, the police-- the Ukrainian police-- they buried them.
They covered them.
OK.
